body:
## Decision
approve

## Differentiation thesis
This should be our conversion wedge only if onboarding is story-aware, not generic product-tour onboarding. Requiring a first chapter + first scene + explicit session objective creates immediate writing momentum and a stronger first-session outcome than template-only starts.

## Competitor evidence
- LivingWriter emphasizes one-click templates/outlines and manuscript creation, which lowers setup friction but still centers structure selection over guided first-session completion: https://livingwriter.com/writing-templates and https://guides.livingwriter.com/product-documentation/mobile-app-guide/dashboard/how-to-create-a-manuscript
- Campfire highlights default templates and modular setup breadth; strong for planning, but can increase initial complexity for new writers: https://campfirewriting.com/ and https://campfirewriting.com/story-planner
- Inkitt positions itself around reader feedback/community loops rather than private drafting activation flow, leaving room for us to win on first-session authoring completion: https://www.inkitt.com/writers and https://www.inkitt.com/guidelines

## Risks / trade-offs
- Over-guiding may frustrate experienced writers even with skip options if prompts feel mandatory.
- Added onboarding surface area can hurt activation if it delays time-to-first-sentence.
- Instrumentation complexity can create noisy metrics unless completion and skip paths are cleanly separated.

## Recommendation
Proceed, but enforce a strict differentiation bar in design: optimize for "first meaningful scene drafted" within 2 minutes, with one-click skip always visible. Keep copy and UI focused on story outcomes (scene objective completion), not feature education.

## Handoff
Assign to: Design
